skill.name: remotion-to-hyperframes
skill.description: Translate an existing Remotion (React-based) video composition into a HyperFrames HTML composition. Use ONLY when the user explicitly asks to port, convert, migrate, translate, or rewrite a Remotion composition as HyperFrames (e.g. "port my Remotion project to HyperFrames"). Do NOT use when (a) authoring a NEW HyperFrames composition (even if A/B-testing a Remotion video); (b) Remotion is mentioned in passing; (c) Remotion code is shared as reference, not for translation; (d) the user wants "the same video as my Remotion one" without explicitly asking to migrate the source — treat as a fresh HyperFrames build. When in doubt, default to the `hyperframes` skill. Detects unsupported patterns (useState, useEffect side effects, async calculateMetadata, third-party React component libraries, `@remotion/lambda`) and recommends the runtime interop escape hatch instead of a lossy translation.
skill.summary: Port Remotion React video compositions to HyperFrames HTML
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate code translation skill that converts Remotion React video compositions to HyperFrames HTML. The extensive static findings (1025 patterns) are primarily located in test infrastructure (assets/test-corpus/), documentation references, and test fixtures - not in the skill's execution path. The skill reads Remotion code and generates HyperFrames HTML output. Shell command patterns detected in markdown files represent documented syntax examples, not actual execution. No malicious intent or user data exfiltration patterns detected.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Patterns in Test Infrastructure
medium_findings.description: Ruby/shell backtick execution patterns detected in assets/test-corpus/ files, scripts/, and references/ documentation. These are test fixtures, documentation examples, and test corpus files - not skill execution paths. The skill itself does not execute these patterns at runtime.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern detected in test infrastructure and documentation files. The skill is a code translator that generates HTML output - it does not execute shell commands at runtime.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Patterns in Test Data
low_findings.description: Path traversal sequences (../) detected in test corpus files and documentation examples. These represent legitimate path references in test fixtures for testing the skill's handling of import paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are test fixture files demonstrating import path handling - not user-controllable paths exploited at runtime.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance in Test Scripts
low_findings.description: Commands like `which` for tool detection found in test scripts. These are legitimate checks for development tool availability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard tool availability checks in development/test scripts - common practice for build systems.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Algorithm References in Test Data
low_findings.description: Weak cryptographic algorithm mentions in expected.json test data files. These are test assertions for evaluating skill outputs against known-good examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Test data containing algorithm names for comparison purposes - not actual cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Hardcoded CDN and documentation URLs in reference files. These are legitimate resource references for font loading and documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard documentation of external font resources - no credential or sensitive data involved.

content.value_statement: Manually converting Remotion video compositions to HyperFrames takes hours. This skill automates the translation by analyzing Remotion React code and generating equivalent HyperFrames HTML with proper sequencing, timing, and transitions.
actual_capabilities: Translate Remotion root components (Title, Scene, Outro) to HyperFrames scene structure
Convert Remotion @-series/sequencing API to HyperFrames scene composition with duration and easing
Transform Remotion timing (useCurrentFrame, interpolate) to HyperFrames CSS animations and WAAPI
Port Remotion Spring and easing configs to equivalent HyperFrames CSS/JS animation definitions
Detect unsupported patterns (useState, useEffect, async metadata, third-party React libraries) and recommend escape hatch approach
Handle data-driven compositions by mapping Remotion props to HyperFrames HTML template variables
limitations: Cannot translate React hooks with stateful side effects (useState, useEffect) - requires escape hatch
Third-party React component libraries cannot be ported automatically
Remotion Lambda serverless rendering is not supported
Advanced audio mixing and video composition require manual work after translation

use_cases.title: Migrate production Remotion library to HyperFrames
use_cases.description: Marketing teams with existing Remotion video templates can port them to HyperFrames for simplified HTML-based rendering without React dependencies.

use_cases.title: Automate Remotion-to-HyperFrames conversion for A/B testing
use_cases.description: Generate both Remotion and HyperFrames versions of a video composition to compare rendering performance and quality.

use_cases.title: Quick HyperFrames prototypes from Remotion examples
use_cases.description: Take community Remotion examples and quickly adapt them to HyperFrames for integration into existing web applications.

prompt_templates.title: Simple title card translation
prompt_templates.prompt: Port this Remotion composition to HyperFrames: [paste Remotion code]
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a single Remotion component to convert

prompt_templates.title: Multi-scene video translation
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my Remotion project at ./src/index.ts to HyperFrames. The composition uses multiple scenes with spring animations.
prompt_templates.scenario: When translating a full Remotion project with scene composition

prompt_templates.title: Translation with unsupported patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Port this Remotion code but flag any useState, useEffect, or async metadata that needs escape hatch handling: [paste code]
prompt_templates.scenario: When the Remotion code may have patterns that cannot be directly translated

prompt_templates.title: Batch translation with evaluation
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Remotion codebase for migration feasibility to HyperFrames. List all components, identify supported patterns, and flag unsupported ones that need escape hatch or manual work.
prompt_templates.scenario: For large codebases requiring assessment before migration

output_examples.input: Translate a Remotion title card with spring animation
output_examples.output: A HyperFrames HTML file with scene composition, CSS keyframe animation matching the spring easing, and proper font loading for Inter font family.
Multiple HTML scene files (TitleScene.html, ContentScene.html, OutroScene.html) with a main composition file that sequences them with durations and transitions.

best_practices: Always review translated output for visual fidelity - some animation curves may need manual tuning
Use the escape hatch for complex React state patterns instead of forcing lossy translations
Test translated videos across browsers - CSS animation timing can vary between browsers
anti_patterns: Do not force translation of useState/useEffect patterns - these require escape hatch approach
Avoid translating @remotion/lambda code - this serverless pattern has no HyperFrames equivalent
Do not attempt to port third-party React component libraries - these must be reimplemented in HTML/CSS/JS

faq.question: Can I use this skill to automatically translate any Remotion video?
faq.answer: No. The skill translates supported patterns like sequencing, timing, and springs. React hooks with stateful side effects, third-party React libraries, and async metadata require manual work or escape hatch approach.

faq.question: What happens to my Remotion code after translation?
faq.answer: The skill generates new HyperFrames HTML files. Your original Remotion code remains unchanged. You will need to maintain both if you want to keep Remotion as an option.

faq.question: How do I handle unsupported Remotion patterns?
faq.answer: The skill detects unsupported patterns and recommends the escape hatch approach. This involves wrapping unsupported React components in iframe or using HyperFrames runtime interop to call the original Remotion code.

faq.question: Does the translated video look exactly like the original?
faq.answer: Mostly, but some animation curves and timing may need manual adjustment. CSS animations and WAAPI approximate Remotion's spring physics with slight variations.

faq.question: Can I run Remotion and HyperFrames side by side?
faq.answer: Yes. HyperFrames renders pure HTML which can coexist with React applications. You can A/B test both versions for performance comparison.

faq.question: What is the escape hatch pattern?
faq.answer: When direct translation is not possible, the escape hatch lets you embed unsupported React code in iframes or use runtime interop to call Remotion from HyperFrames. This preserves functionality at the cost of added complexity.
